text: The surgeon inserts a zero-degree telescope through the umbilical port to provide a laparoscopic view of the surgical field.

The surgical team places two working ports on the right side of the abdomen: a 10mm port in the right iliac fossa at the ileostomy site and a 5mm port in the right upper quadrant above the umbilicus, both aligned in the midclavicular line with the right port slightly lateral. Two 5mm assistant grasping ports are positioned in the left iliac fossa and left upper abdomen. An optional 5mm suprapubic port is available for retraction during rectal dissection.
The surgeon carefully moves the small bowel loops out of the pelvic cavity, clearing the surgical field for further dissection.
The surgeon lifts the sigmoid mesocolon, visibly stretching it, while also stretching the mesorectum.
The surgeon identifies the right ureter and stretches the pedicle of the inferior mesenteric artery and superior hemorrhoidal artery.
The surgeon makes an incision on the peritoneum starting at the sacral promontory in the midline, extending downwards onto the mesorectum and upwards parallel to the aorta.

The surgeon identifies the vascular pedicle of the inferior mesenteric artery and carefully dissects it free from the surrounding areolar tissue. The sympathetic nerve trunk is also identified and preserved during the dissection.
The surgeon further dissects and skeletonizes the inferior mesenteric artery, applying metallic or locking clips before dividing the artery.
The surgeon performs blunt dissection in the posterior avascular plane, separating the sigmoid mesocolon from the underlying retroperitoneal structures.
The surgeon identifies and dissects the ureter and gonadal vessels posteriorly, carefully separating these structures from surrounding tissues during the dissection.

The surgeon extends the dissection laterally to reach the lateral peritoneal attachment, known as the white line of Toldt. Superiorly, the dissection progresses to mobilize the splenic flexure of the colon.
The surgeon performs rectal dissection in the pre-sacral avascular plane, taking care to safeguard the left ureter during the medial to lateral approach.
The surgeon continues the rectal dissection posteriorly in the avascular plane, carefully separating the rectum from surrounding tissues without encountering blood vessels.

The surgeon divides the right lateral peritoneal folds, ensuring minimal bleeding by staying in the correct avascular plane. The mesorectum is removed along with the rectum, while carefully safeguarding the hypogastric sympathetic nerves from injury during the dissection.
The surgeon divides the peritoneal fold anterior to the rectum within the rectovaginal pouch in females and the rectovesical pouch in males, carefully separating these structures.

The surgeon continues the dissection posteriorly in the presacral space, advancing towards the pelvic floor. The right lateral ligaments are divided to further separate the rectum from the pelvic cavity.

The surgeon separates the rectum anteriorly from the vagina in females and from the prostate and seminal vesicle in males, continuing the dissection down to the pelvic floor. Adequate retraction and traction are applied to facilitate the rectal dissection.

The surgeon divides the left peritoneal attachments and left lateral ligaments, progressing the dissection down to the pelvic floor. The anterior dissection is completed, revealing the rectum separated from surrounding structures.
The surgeon divides the lateral peritoneal attachments of the sigmoid colon and descending colon, progressing towards the splenic flexure of the colon.
The surgeon completes the mobilization of the splenic flexure by detaching it from its peritoneal and retroperitoneal attachments, ensuring the colon is free from surrounding structures.

The surgeon mobilizes the ileocecal junction from its lateral peritoneal attachment, progressing upwards to the hepatic flexure of the colon. The hepatic flexure is fully mobilized, allowing for its complete detachment.

The surgeon uses roticulating 45mm endocutting staplers to divide the rectum at the pelvic floor, ensuring the division is flush to retain a small cuff for a double stapled anastomosis. Typically, two cartridges are used to complete the division.
A small incision, approximately five to six centimeters, is made in the midline of the peri-umbilical region. The surgeon delivers the mobilized rectum through this mini laparotomy incision.
The surgeon divides the transverse mesocolon from the transverse colon, ligating and dividing all vascular pedicles supplying the colon.

The surgeon divides the ileocecal junction near the IC junction and closes the terminal ileum in two layers. Stay sutures are placed on the folded terminal ileum to form an ileal J pouch measuring fifteen centimeters in length.
The surgeon opens the ileum and uses two fires of linear cutting staplers, measuring seventy-five or eighty millimeters, to construct an ileal J pouch of fifteen centimeters in length.

The surgeon inserts the circular stapler anvil and secures it with a purse string suture. The ileal J pouch is placed back into the abdomen, and the abdominal wall is closed to recreate the neoperitoneum.

The surgeon passes a circular stapler through the rectum, piercing the rectal cuff. Under telescopic vision, the anvil is approximated into the circular stapler. The ileal J pouch is carefully aligned with the rectal cuff, ensuring the pouch and its mesentery are properly positioned without tension on the stapler line. The stapler is fired, creating the J pouch anal anastomosis.
The surgeon checks the anastomosis for leakage, performs a proximal diverting ileostomy, and closes the mini laparotomy wound to complete the colectomy procedure.
